articles.title: North Charleston, SC home values, income, and owner costs
articles.dek: A borrower-focused reading of the latest ACS value, income, and owner-cost evidence for North Charleston, SC.
articles.previewText: See how home value, household income, and owner-cost estimates frame a payment conversation in North Charleston, SC.
articles.metaDescription: North Charleston, SC home values, income, and owner costs. See how home value, household income, and owner-cost estimates frame a payment conversation in North.
keyTakeaways: prior ACS median owner-occupied home value: $174,800.
median monthly owner cost with a mortgage: $1,652.
change from the prior ACS home-value estimate: 72.9%.

evidenceFacts.label: prior ACS median owner-occupied home value
median monthly owner cost with a mortgage
change from the prior ACS home-value estimate
local median home-value gap above the state estimate

sections.heading: The local value, income, and owner-cost baseline
body: In North Charleston, SC, the 2024 ACS reports a median owner-occupied home value of $302,200 and median household income of $62,956; these are separate medians, not the finances of one representative household. The same release reports $1,652 in median selected monthly owner costs for owners with a mortgage, a measure that combines recurring ownership expenses rather than real estate taxes alone. For North Charleston, SC, reading $302,200, $62,956, and $1,652 together identifies the cost categories that deserve verification without converting public medians into a payment quote. An affordability review can start with the local $302,200, $62,956, and $1,652 reference points, then replace them with the borrower's income, cash, debts, property taxes, insurance, association charges, loan terms, and intended occupancy.

sections.heading: What changed between the ACS periods
body: North Charleston, SC's median home-value estimate increased from $174,800 in the prior ACS period to $302,200 in the current release, a reported change of 72.9%. Because each ACS 5-year estimate summarizes a multi-year survey window, the 72.9% comparison describes a change between published estimates rather than appreciation for a particular home. The move from $174,800 to $302,200 can help a borrower test whether an older search budget still reflects the broad local evidence, but it cannot identify a current asking price or future resale value in North Charleston, SC. Before the 72.9% change is treated as precise, the analysis still needs margins of error, property type, neighborhood, condition, and the timing of an actual transaction.

sections.heading: How the city estimate sits within the state
body: North Charleston, SC's $302,200 median home-value estimate is above the statewide $259,000 estimate, with a reported city-to-state gap of 16.7%. That 16.7% gap is useful for showing that a statewide midpoint may be a poor substitute for a local search range in North Charleston, SC. It does not mean every local property is 16.7% away from a comparable property elsewhere in the state, because the medians reflect different housing mixes and geographies. A borrower can use $302,200 and $259,000 to decide which assumptions need local replacement first, then compare actual properties and current financing disclosures on the same basis.

sections.heading: What the owner-cost estimate includes and excludes
body: The $1,652 owner-cost estimate gives North Charleston, SC borrowers a broader monthly reference than principal and interest alone, while the $62,956 income estimate supplies separate household context. The two medians should not be divided to claim that a typical household spends a particular share of income, because ACS does not say the household at $62,956 is the owner household at $1,652. The $302,200 local home-value estimate adds market context, but it also cannot supply a down payment, interest rate, mortgage insurance amount, tax bill, or insurance quote. Keeping $302,200, $62,956, and $1,652 in their published roles prevents a local affordability worksheet from acquiring false precision before a property and borrower are known.

sections.heading: What these local medians cannot decide
body: Neither $302,200 nor $1,652 determines the value of a specific North Charleston, SC home, what a borrower will pay each month, or an underwriting result. The $62,956 median describes the local household distribution, not the stability, continuity, documentation, or eligible amount of an applicant's income. Even the observed 72.9% between ACS periods cannot forecast the next market move or establish a safe purchase horizon. Those boundaries keep the accurate $302,200, $1,652, and $62,956 statistics from being misused as an appraisal, rate quote, underwriting result, or recommendation about how much debt one household should carry.

sections.heading: Build a property-specific comparison
body: A practical North Charleston, SC worksheet can begin with $302,200 as broad value context, $1,652 as a reminder to include recurring ownership expenses, and $62,956 as a local economic reference. After noting those $302,200, $1,652, and $62,956 medians, substitute a real property price, down-payment plan, current loan disclosures, taxes, insurance, association obligations, maintenance allowance, and cash reserves. The 16.7% city-to-state difference is a prompt to keep the analysis local, while the 72.9% period change is a prompt to date every assumption. A licensed professional can use the 16.7% and 72.9% comparisons to explain which inputs are verified, which may change, and which loan options fit the documented borrower and property without presenting the ACS figures as an outcome.

articles.methodology: This article uses 2024 and 2019 ACS 5-year estimates for the Census place and a 2024 parent-state comparison, including published margins of error. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: ACS estimates represent survey periods rather than point-in-time prices, and apparent changes may not be statistically meaningful without a margin-of-error test. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.

articles.title: North Charleston, SC housing supply, ownership, and rent
articles.dek: What the latest ACS housing mix can and cannot tell buyers and renters comparing options in North Charleston, SC.
articles.previewText: Review housing units, occupancy, tenure, vacancy, and rent evidence before comparing buying and renting in North Charleston, SC.
articles.metaDescription: North Charleston, SC housing supply, ownership, and rent. Review housing units, occupancy, tenure, vacancy, and rent evidence before comparing buying and.

evidenceFacts.label: change in vacant units from the prior ACS period

sections.heading: The size and use of the local housing stock
body: North Charleston, SC's current ACS housing baseline includes 54,781 total housing units, 49,185 occupied units, and 5,596 vacant units. Those counts describe the housing stock across the survey period; they do not say that 5,596 homes were listed for sale or available to rent at one moment. The difference between 54,781 total units and 49,185 occupied units is useful for checking the internal scale of the release, while 5,596 provides the numerator for a more careful vacancy reading. For a borrower researching North Charleston, SC, the counts establish how large the measured housing base is before tenure, rent, current listings, property condition, and financing questions are added.

sections.heading: Vacancy changed, but it is not listing inventory
body: Vacant units in North Charleston, SC increased from 5,192 in the prior ACS period to 5,596 in the current release, a reported change of 7.8%. In the current housing stock, 5,596 units equal 10.2% of the 54,781 total. ACS vacancy covers several statuses, so the 10.2% share cannot be read as the share of homes a buyer can purchase today or the share of rentals immediately available. The movement from 5,192 to 5,596 can still prompt better questions about current for-sale and rental inventory in North Charleston, SC, provided those questions are answered with timely listing and property data rather than the survey count alone.

sections.heading: Ownership and renting within occupied housing
body: Among 49,185 occupied units in North Charleston, SC, the ACS-derived owner share is 49.8% and the renter share is 50.2%, making renter-occupied housing the larger measured tenure group. The 49.8% and 50.2% figures describe how occupied units were distributed; they do not measure how many households are ready to buy, how many owners plan to sell, or which properties can qualify for a mortgage. This local tenure mix helps a borrower avoid assuming that one housing path dominates every segment of North Charleston, SC. The 49.8% and 50.2% mix is most useful when paired with actual choices at a similar property type, location, condition, and time horizon rather than used as a signal to buy or rent by itself.

sections.heading: Use rent as a matched comparison, not a shortcut
body: North Charleston, SC's ACS median gross rent is $1,430, while renter-occupied units account for 50.2% of occupied housing and owner-occupied units account for 49.8%. The $1,430 median includes rent and certain utilities for the measured rental stock; it does not identify a lease offer comparable to a home under consideration. Comparing $1,430 with only a mortgage's principal and interest would omit taxes, insurance, maintenance, association charges, transaction costs, and differences between the properties. A more useful North Charleston, SC comparison matches real lease and ownership choices over the same expected horizon, then treats the tenure shares as market context instead of a break-even calculation.

sections.heading: What the housing-stock evidence cannot show
body: The 10.2% vacant-unit share and 49.8% owner share cannot determine competition for a specific North Charleston, SC listing, seller flexibility, inspection risk, appraisal results, insurance availability, or future supply. Likewise, 54,781 total units say nothing about how many properties fit a borrower's price, location, accessibility, unit-count, or condition requirements. The ACS period behind the 10.2% vacancy share can accurately describe the broad stock while lagging a fast-moving listing market. Borrowers should therefore keep 5,596 vacant units separate from active inventory and keep the $1,430 median separate from a current lease quote, using each figure only for the question its definition can answer.

sections.heading: Compare current local choices on the same terms
body: A grounded North Charleston, SC comparison starts with current listings and lease offers, then uses the ACS evidence to check whether the sample is being interpreted in the context of 54,781 total units, a 10.2% vacancy share, and a 49.8% owner share. For renting, replace the $1,430 median with the actual lease, utilities, deposits, and expected changes. For ownership, keep the 49.8% share as market context while adding the selected property's price, taxes, insurance, maintenance, association obligations, loan disclosures, and cash-to-close. The resulting worksheet will not turn 50.2% or 49.8% into a recommendation, but it will keep the two paths comparable and make the remaining assumptions visible before a decision.

articles.methodology: This article uses 2024 ACS 5-year place estimates for units, occupancy, vacancy, owner and renter tenure, and gross rent, with 2019 evidence retained for comparison review. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: ACS vacancy includes categories beyond for-sale availability, and tenure and rent estimates carry sampling error and do not measure current listing inventory. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.

articles.title: North Charleston, SC labor market: latest employment evidence
articles.dek: A borrower-focused reading of the latest BLS labor-force, employment, unemployment, and rate measures for North Charleston, SC.
articles.previewText: See what changed in North Charleston, SC, SC's labor market and why broad employment data cannot determine mortgage eligibility.
articles.metaDescription: North Charleston, SC labor market: latest employment evidence. See what changed in North Charleston, SC, SC's labor market and why broad employment data cannot.

evidenceFacts.label: change in unemployment rate from the prior month
change in unemployment rate from a year earlier

sections.heading: The latest labor-force counts
body: The latest LAUS release reports a North Charleston, SC labor force of 69,487, with 67,215 people employed and 2,272 unemployed under the program's definitions. Those three counts belong together: 67,215 plus 2,272 explains the scale behind the published 3.3% unemployment rate. For mortgage planning, the 69,487 city-level labor force describes the surrounding market rather than the income or employment record of an applicant. A borrower can use the 69,487 labor-force baseline to understand the size of the measured market, but qualification still depends on documented income, employment continuity, debts, assets, and the rules of the loan being considered.

sections.heading: What changed from the prior month
body: North Charleston, SC's unemployment rate rose from 3.0% in the prior month to 3.3% in the latest month, a change of 0.3%. A 0.3% move can reflect current conditions, normal sampling movement, and later revisions, so it should be read with the underlying 2,272 unemployment count and 69,487 labor force. The 0.3% comparison does not show that a particular employer is hiring or cutting jobs, and it cannot predict the next release. For a North Charleston, SC borrower, the monthly change is a prompt to date economic assumptions and verify personal employment documentation, not a reason to assume approval, denial, or a particular loan price.

sections.heading: The year-over-year comparison
body: Compared with 3.3% a year earlier, the latest North Charleston, SC unemployment rate of 3.3% was unchanged by 0.0%. The year-over-year window reduces the risk of interpreting only one monthly step, but the 0.0% difference still describes a population estimate rather than an individual's job stability. Reading 3.3%, 3.0%, and 3.3% together shows whether the latest month sits within a wider movement without forecasting what follows. A borrower should keep that broad North Charleston, SC pattern separate from pay history, variable compensation, self-employment records, contracts, leave, job changes, and other facts a lender may need to document.

sections.heading: Why the rate needs its underlying counts
body: At 3.3%, North Charleston, SC's unemployment rate summarizes 2,272 unemployed people within a 69,487 labor force; the rate should not be discussed without those counts. The 67,215 employment estimate is an economic measure and does not distinguish borrowers by hours, tenure, pay structure, industry, or the documentation available for a loan file. That makes 67,215 useful for local context but unsuitable as evidence that one household's income is stable or sufficient. Keeping the 3.3% rate attached to 69,487, 67,215, and 2,272 prevents a small percentage movement from carrying more borrower-level meaning than the LAUS release supports.

sections.heading: Labor statistics are not an income review
body: Neither 67,215 employed people nor a 3.3% unemployment rate tells a lender what one North Charleston, SC applicant earns, whether that income is eligible, or whether it is likely to continue. The 0.3% monthly rate change also cannot substitute for pay stubs, tax returns, business records, contracts, benefit documentation, or an explanation of a recent job transition. The 3.3% rate can be relevant to timing and local economic questions while remaining outside the underwriting decision. That boundary protects borrowers from treating a favorable 0.0% year-over-year change as an approval signal or an unfavorable movement as a conclusion about their own documented capacity.

sections.heading: Turn the trend into documentation questions
body: A practical next step for a North Charleston, SC borrower is to note the latest 3.3% unemployment rate and the 0.0% year-over-year change, then move immediately to the records that describe the household. After noting the 3.3% rate, compare current base pay, variable earnings, self-employment history, contracts, assets, debts, and expected changes with the documentation requirements for the loan path. The 69,487 local labor force and 67,215 employment count can frame the economic conversation, but they should not alter verified borrower inputs. The 0.3% monthly movement remains a population measure even when it appears consistent with a borrower's personal experience. A licensed professional can explain which documents and time periods matter while keeping North Charleston, SC's 3.3% LAUS rate in its proper role as dated local economic context.

articles.methodology: This article uses BLS LAUS unadjusted city monthly series for labor force, employment, unemployment, and unemployment rate, with prior-month and year-ago comparisons. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: LAUS estimates can be revised, city series are unadjusted, and local labor statistics describe a population rather than an individual applicant's income or employment. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.

articles.title: North Charleston, SC loan limits: 2026 conforming and FHA context
articles.dek: How 2026 conforming and FHA loan-limit figures organize conventional, jumbo, and FHA questions without determining eligibility.
articles.previewText: Compare 2026 conforming and FHA loan-limit figures associated with North Charleston, SC, including unit-count differences and important limits.
articles.metaDescription: North Charleston, SC loan limits: 2026 conforming and FHA context. Compare 2026 conforming and FHA loan-limit figures associated with North Charleston, SC.
sourceLabels: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development
keyTakeaways: difference between one-unit conforming and FHA limits: $142,750.

evidenceFacts.label: difference between one-unit conforming and FHA limits

sections.heading: The published one-unit limits for the matched area
body: North Charleston, SC is connected to Charleston County for this 2026 source comparison. The published one-unit conforming limit is $832,750, while the published one-unit FHA limit is $690,000. Those figures organize different program categories; $832,750 is not a conventional loan offer, and $690,000 is not an FHA eligibility finding. For North Charleston, SC, starting with Charleston County keeps the limits attached to the county or county-equivalent area used in the official files. A North Charleston, SC borrower still needs to confirm the selected property's location and legal unit count, because the relevant Charleston County row follows the property rather than the city label or mailing address alone. Around North Charleston, SC, mailing cities and ZIP codes can cross county boundaries, so the official property jurisdiction should be verified instead of inferred from a familiar place name. Keep Charleston County as North Charleston, SC's confirmed county row before comparing the $832,750 and $690,000 program boundaries.

sections.heading: How the one-unit program boundaries compare
body: For the matched North Charleston, SC area, the difference between the $832,750 one-unit conforming limit and the $690,000 one-unit FHA limit is $142,750. The $142,750 gap marks different published program boundaries, not a pricing advantage or a recommendation to borrow more. A needed loan amount can be compared with $832,750 and $690,000 only after price, down payment, financed items, and the applicable base-loan definition are established. Even when $142,750 is material, it cannot determine approval, mortgage insurance, cash requirements, rates, fees, or whether either program suits the borrower.

sections.heading: Unit count changes the published ceiling
body: Charleston County's conforming limit rises from $832,750 for one unit to $1,601,750 for four units, while the FHA figures move from $690,000 to $1,326,950. The higher $1,601,750 and $1,326,950 amounts apply to the published four-unit category; they do not apply to a one-unit home merely because a borrower wants a larger loan. In North Charleston, SC, a legal unit count should be verified from reliable property information before a multi-unit limit is used. The $1,601,750 and $1,326,950 ceilings do not resolve occupancy, appraisal, rental-income treatment, reserves, or other multi-unit program rules, so the amount in the table is only one part of the product discussion.

sections.heading: A limit is a category boundary, not borrowing power
body: The $832,750 conforming ceiling and $690,000 FHA ceiling classify a loan amount within their respective 2026 county tables; neither amount increases a North Charleston, SC household's income or reduces its debts. A borrower seeking less than $832,750 can still have important conventional requirements to review, and a request below $690,000 can still require FHA-specific eligibility, property, mortgage-insurance, and occupancy analysis. The $142,750 program gap is therefore best used to route questions, not to rank products. Regardless of the $142,750 gap, borrowing capacity comes from the documented borrower, property, transaction, and current underwriting rules rather than the largest number available in a public limit file.

sections.heading: What the county limits cannot decide
body: Neither $1,601,750 nor $1,326,950 indicates what a four-unit property in North Charleston, SC is worth, what income its units may produce, or whether a borrower can qualify to purchase it. The one-unit $832,750 and $690,000 limits likewise cannot establish a down payment, debt-to-income result, appraisal, reserves, mortgage insurance, interest rate, fees, or final approval. The $832,750 and $690,000 official limits can change by year, and a corrected property geography or unit count can change the applicable row. That is why North Charleston, SC's connection to Charleston County and the 2026 period must remain visible whenever these figures are carried into a planning worksheet or product conversation.

sections.heading: Verify the property before comparing products
body: A useful North Charleston, SC next step is to document the property address, Charleston County geography, legal unit count, price, down-payment plan, and expected base loan amount. Compare that amount with $832,750 and $690,000 for a one-unit property, or with $1,601,750 and $1,326,950 only when the four-unit category is actually applicable. After the $832,750 and $690,000 comparison, ask a licensed professional to verify the current official row and explain the borrower, property, occupancy, insurance, and documentation rules attached to each option. The $832,750 conforming figure and $690,000 FHA figure should remain dated to 2026 throughout that review. This sequence uses the $142,750 one-unit gap as planning context while avoiding any promise that a published ceiling is available, affordable, or appropriate.

Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: The location mapping identifies a published source geography for this location record, but a specific property can require separate geography verification and current program guidance. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.
